title: Limit Definition (Epsilon-Delta)
content: The limit of f(x) as x approaches a is L if for every epsilon > 0 there exists delta > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < epsilon whenever 0 < |x - a| < delta. This rigorous definition formalizes the intuitive notion that f(x) gets arbitrarily close to L as x gets close to a.
formula_latex: \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L \iff \forall \epsilon > 0,\ \exists \delta > 0 : 0 < |x - a| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x) - L| < \epsilon
example: Find lim(x->2) (x^2 - 4)/(x - 2). Factor the numerator: (x+2)(x-2)/(x-2). Cancel (x-2) to get (x+2). Substitute x=2: 2+2 = 4. So the limit is 4.

title: L'Hopital's Rule
content: L'Hopital's Rule states that if lim f(x)/g(x) gives an indeterminate form 0/0 or infinity/infinity, then the limit equals lim f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter limit exists. The rule can be applied repeatedly if the result is still indeterminate.
formula_latex: \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)} \quad \text{when } \frac{0}{0} \text{ or } \frac{\infty}{\infty}
example: Find lim(x->0) sin(x)/x. This is 0/0 form. Apply L'Hopital: lim(x->0) cos(x)/1 = cos(0) = 1.

title: Standard Limit: sin(x)/x
content: The limit of sin(x)/x as x approaches 0 equals 1. This is one of the most fundamental standard limits in calculus. It is proved using the Squeeze Theorem with the geometric inequality sin(x) < x < tan(x) for 0 < x < pi/2.
formula_latex: \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x} = 1
example: Find lim(x->0) sin(5x)/(3x). Rewrite as (5/3) * sin(5x)/(5x). As x->0, 5x->0, so sin(5x)/(5x)->1. Answer: 5/3.

title: Standard Limit: (1 + 1/n)^n and Exponential Limits
content: The limit of (1 + 1/n)^n as n approaches infinity equals e (Euler's number, approximately 2.71828). A useful generalization is lim(x->0) (1 + x)^(1/x) = e. These limits appear frequently in compound interest, growth models, and JEE problems.
formula_latex: \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^n = e \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{e^x - 1}{x} = 1
example: Find lim(x->0) (e^(3x) - 1)/x. Rewrite as 3 * (e^(3x) - 1)/(3x). Let t = 3x; as x->0, t->0. So lim = 3 * 1 = 3.

title: Squeeze Theorem (Sandwich Theorem)
content: If g(x) <= f(x) <= h(x) for all x near a (except possibly at a), and lim g(x) = lim h(x) = L as x->a, then lim f(x) = L. This theorem is essential when direct evaluation is difficult but bounding functions are available.
formula_latex: g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x) \text{ and } \lim_{x \to a} g(x) = \lim_{x \to a} h(x) = L \implies \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L
example: Find lim(x->0) x^2 * sin(1/x). Since -1 <= sin(1/x) <= 1, we have -x^2 <= x^2 sin(1/x) <= x^2. Both -x^2 and x^2 tend to 0 as x->0. By Squeeze Theorem, the limit is 0.

title: Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes
content: To evaluate limits as x approaches infinity for rational functions, divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x. If degree of numerator < denominator, limit is 0. If degrees are equal, limit is the ratio of leading coefficients. If degree of numerator > denominator, the limit is infinity.
formula_latex: \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{a_n x^n + \cdots}{b_m x^m + \cdots} = \begin{cases} 0 & n < m \\ \frac{a_n}{b_m} & n = m \\ \pm\infty & n > m \end{cases}
example: Find lim(x->infinity) (3x^2 + 5x)/(7x^2 - 2). Divide by x^2: (3 + 5/x)/(7 - 2/x^2). As x->infinity, 5/x->0 and 2/x^2->0. Limit = 3/7.

title: Standard Limit: log(1+x)/x
content: The limit of ln(1+x)/x as x approaches 0 is 1. More generally, lim(x->0) log_a(1+x)/x = 1/ln(a). This standard limit is frequently used in JEE problems involving logarithmic expressions.
formula_latex: \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\ln(1+x)}{x} = 1
example: Find lim(x->0) ln(1+3x)/(2x). Rewrite as (3/2) * ln(1+3x)/(3x). Let t = 3x; as x->0, t->0, and ln(1+t)/t -> 1. Answer: 3/2.

title: Definition of Continuity at a Point
content: A function f(x) is continuous at x = a if three conditions hold: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim(x->a) f(x) exists, and (3) lim(x->a) f(x) = f(a). If any condition fails, f is discontinuous at a. All polynomial functions are continuous everywhere.
formula_latex: f \text{ is continuous at } x = a \iff \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)
example: Check continuity of f(x) = (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) at x = 1. f(1) is undefined (0/0), so condition (1) fails. f is discontinuous at x = 1. However, lim(x->1) f(x) = lim(x->1) (x+1) = 2 exists, so it is a removable discontinuity.

title: Types of Discontinuity
content: Discontinuities are classified as: (1) Removable discontinuity: limit exists but does not equal f(a) or f(a) is undefined. (2) Jump discontinuity: left-hand and right-hand limits exist but are not equal. (3) Infinite discontinuity: at least one of the one-sided limits is infinite. (4) Oscillatory discontinuity: the function oscillates without settling (e.g., sin(1/x) at x=0).
formula_latex: \text{Jump: } \lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) \neq \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x), \quad \text{Removable: } \lim_{x \to a} f(x) \neq f(a)
example: f(x) = |x|/x has a jump discontinuity at x = 0. Left-hand limit = -1, right-hand limit = +1. Since -1 != 1, the limit does not exist and the discontinuity is a jump.

title: Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
content: If f is continuous on [a, b] and k is any value between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f(c) = k. A key corollary: if f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, there is at least one root in (a, b). IVT is used to prove existence of roots.
formula_latex: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b],\ f(a) < k < f(b) \implies \exists\, c \in (a,b) : f(c) = k
example: Show x^3 - x - 1 = 0 has a root in (1, 2). Let f(x) = x^3 - x - 1. f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0. f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 > 0. Since f is continuous and changes sign, by IVT there is a root c in (1, 2).

title: Derivative from First Principles
content: The derivative of f(x) at x = a is defined as the limit of the difference quotient: f'(a) = lim(h->0) [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h, provided this limit exists. Geometrically, it represents the slope of the tangent line at (a, f(a)). This is also called differentiation from first principles.
formula_latex: f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}
example: Find d/dx(x^2) from first principles. f'(x) = lim(h->0) [(x+h)^2 - x^2]/h = lim(h->0) [2xh + h^2]/h = lim(h->0) (2x + h) = 2x.

title: Power Rule for Differentiation
content: If f(x) = x^n where n is any real number, then f'(x) = n*x^(n-1). This rule extends to negative and fractional exponents. Combined with the constant multiple rule, d/dx[c*x^n] = c*n*x^(n-1).
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = n x^{n-1} \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{R}
example: Find d/dx(x^5 - 3x^3 + 7x). Using power rule term by term: 5x^4 - 9x^2 + 7. For fractional exponents: d/dx(sqrt(x)) = d/dx(x^(1/2)) = (1/2)x^(-1/2) = 1/(2*sqrt(x)).

title: Chain Rule
content: If y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). In Leibniz notation, if y = f(u) and u = g(x), then dy/dx = (dy/du)*(du/dx). The chain rule is essential for differentiating composite functions.
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)
example: Find d/dx[sin(3x^2)]. Let u = 3x^2, so d/dx[sin(u)] = cos(u) * du/dx = cos(3x^2) * 6x = 6x*cos(3x^2).

title: Product Rule
content: If u(x) and v(x) are differentiable, then d/dx[u*v] = u'*v + u*v'. In words: derivative of first times second plus first times derivative of second. This extends to three functions: (uvw)' = u'vw + uv'w + uvw'.
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}[u \cdot v] = u' v + u v'
example: Find d/dx[x^2 * sin(x)]. Let u = x^2, v = sin(x). u' = 2x, v' = cos(x). d/dx = 2x*sin(x) + x^2*cos(x).

title: Quotient Rule
content: If u(x) and v(x) are differentiable and v(x) != 0, then d/dx[u/v] = (u'v - uv')/v^2. A mnemonic: 'low d-high minus high d-low, over the square of what's below.'
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{u}{v}\right] = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}
example: Find d/dx[tan(x)] = d/dx[sin(x)/cos(x)]. u = sin(x), v = cos(x), u' = cos(x), v' = -sin(x). d/dx = [cos(x)*cos(x) - sin(x)*(-sin(x))]/cos^2(x) = [cos^2(x) + sin^2(x)]/cos^2(x) = 1/cos^2(x) = sec^2(x).

title: Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions
content: The standard trigonometric derivatives are: d/dx[sin(x)] = cos(x), d/dx[cos(x)] = -sin(x), d/dx[tan(x)] = sec^2(x), d/dx[cot(x)] = -csc^2(x), d/dx[sec(x)] = sec(x)tan(x), d/dx[csc(x)] = -csc(x)cot(x). These are frequently combined with the chain rule in JEE problems.
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}\sin x = \cos x, \quad \frac{d}{dx}\cos x = -\sin x, \quad \frac{d}{dx}\tan x = \sec^2 x
example: Find d/dx[sec(3x)]. Using chain rule: sec(3x)*tan(3x) * d/dx(3x) = 3*sec(3x)*tan(3x).

title: Implicit Differentiation
content: When y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x, y) = 0, we differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as a function of x and applying the chain rule. Every time we differentiate a term involving y, we multiply by dy/dx. Then solve for dy/dx.
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}[F(x,y)] = 0 \implies F_x + F_y \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{F_x}{F_y}
example: Find dy/dx for x^2 + y^2 = 25. Differentiate: 2x + 2y*(dy/dx) = 0. Solve: dy/dx = -x/y. At (3, 4): dy/dx = -3/4.

title: Logarithmic Differentiation
content: For functions of the form y = [f(x)]^g(x) or products/quotients of many terms, take the natural log of both sides, differentiate using properties of logarithms, and solve for dy/dx. This technique simplifies complex expressions by converting products to sums and powers to products.
formula_latex: y = f(x)^{g(x)} \implies \ln y = g(x) \ln f(x) \implies \frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = g'(x)\ln f(x) + g(x)\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}
example: Find dy/dx for y = x^x. Take ln: ln(y) = x*ln(x). Differentiate: (1/y)*(dy/dx) = ln(x) + 1. So dy/dx = x^x * (ln(x) + 1).

title: Equation of Tangent and Normal Lines
content: At a point (x0, y0) on the curve y = f(x), the tangent line has slope m = f'(x0) and equation y - y0 = m(x - x0). The normal line is perpendicular to the tangent with slope -1/m and equation y - y0 = (-1/m)(x - x0).
formula_latex: \text{Tangent: } y - y_0 = f'(x_0)(x - x_0), \quad \text{Normal: } y - y_0 = -\frac{1}{f'(x_0)}(x - x_0)
example: Find the tangent to y = x^3 at (1, 1). f'(x) = 3x^2, f'(1) = 3. Tangent: y - 1 = 3(x - 1), i.e., y = 3x - 2. Normal: y - 1 = (-1/3)(x - 1), i.e., y = -x/3 + 4/3.

title: Maxima and Minima (First Derivative Test)
content: A function f(x) has a local maximum at x = c if f'(c) = 0 and f' changes sign from positive to negative at c. It has a local minimum if f' changes from negative to positive. Points where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) is undefined are called critical points.
formula_latex: f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f' \text{ changes } + \to - \implies \text{local max}; \quad - \to + \implies \text{local min}
example: Find extrema of f(x) = x^3 - 3x + 2. f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 3(x-1)(x+1) = 0 gives x = 1, -1. f' changes +to- at x=-1 (local max, f(-1)=4) and -to+ at x=1 (local min, f(1)=0).

title: Second Derivative Test for Extrema
content: If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0, then f has a local minimum at c. If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0, then f has a local maximum at c. If f''(c) = 0, the test is inconclusive and we must use the first derivative test or higher-order derivatives.
formula_latex: f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f''(c) > 0 \implies \text{local min}; \quad f''(c) < 0 \implies \text{local max}

title: Rate of Change
content: The derivative f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of f with respect to x. In related rates problems, if two quantities are related by an equation and both change with time t, we differentiate the equation with respect to t to find how their rates are related.
formula_latex: \text{Rate of change} = \frac{dy}{dx}, \quad \text{Related rates: } \frac{d}{dt}[F(x,y)] = 0
example: A circle's radius increases at 2 cm/s. Find the rate of area increase when r = 5 cm. A = pi*r^2. dA/dt = 2*pi*r*(dr/dt) = 2*pi*5*2 = 20*pi cm^2/s.

title: Rolle's Theorem
content: If f is continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and f(a) = f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f'(c) = 0. Geometrically, there is a point where the tangent is horizontal. Rolle's theorem is a special case of the Mean Value Theorem.
formula_latex: f(a) = f(b) \implies \exists\, c \in (a,b) : f'(c) = 0
example: Verify Rolle's theorem for f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3 on [1, 3]. f(1) = 1 - 4 + 3 = 0, f(3) = 9 - 12 + 3 = 0. So f(1) = f(3). f'(x) = 2x - 4 = 0 gives x = 2 in (1, 3). Verified.

title: Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem (LMVT)
content: If f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f'(c) = [f(b) - f(a)]/(b - a). This means the instantaneous rate of change at some point equals the average rate of change over the interval.
formula_latex: f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a} \quad \text{for some } c \in (a, b)
example: Verify LMVT for f(x) = x^2 on [1, 3]. f(3) - f(1) = 9 - 1 = 8, b - a = 2, so RHS = 4. f'(x) = 2x = 4 gives x = 2 in (1, 3). Verified.

title: Increasing and Decreasing Functions
content: A function f is increasing on an interval if f'(x) > 0 for all x in that interval, and decreasing if f'(x) < 0. To find intervals of increase/decrease, solve f'(x) = 0 to find critical points, then test the sign of f' in each sub-interval.
formula_latex: f'(x) > 0 \implies f \text{ is increasing}; \quad f'(x) < 0 \implies f \text{ is decreasing}
example: Find where f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1 is increasing. f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x - 1)(x - 3). f'(x) > 0 when x < 1 or x > 3. So f is increasing on (-infinity, 1) and (3, infinity).

title: Antiderivative and Indefinite Integral
content: An antiderivative of f(x) is a function F(x) such that F'(x) = f(x). The indefinite integral of f(x) is the family of all antiderivatives: integral of f(x) dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration. The constant C accounts for all functions with the same derivative.
formula_latex: \int f(x)\, dx = F(x) + C \quad \text{where } F'(x) = f(x)
example: Find integral of (3x^2 + 2x) dx. Antiderivative of 3x^2 is x^3, antiderivative of 2x is x^2. Answer: x^3 + x^2 + C.

title: Power Rule for Integration
content: The integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C for n != -1. When n = -1, integral of x^(-1) dx = integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. This is the reverse of the power rule for differentiation.
formula_latex: \int x^n\, dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} + C \quad (n \neq -1), \quad \int \frac{1}{x}\, dx = \ln|x| + C
example: Find integral of (x^4 + 1/x^2) dx = integral of (x^4 + x^(-2)) dx = x^5/5 + x^(-1)/(-1) + C = x^5/5 - 1/x + C.

title: Standard Integrals of Trigonometric and Exponential Functions
content: Key standard integrals include: integral of sin(x) dx = -cos(x) + C, integral of cos(x) dx = sin(x) + C, integral of sec^2(x) dx = tan(x) + C, integral of csc^2(x) dx = -cot(x) + C, integral of e^x dx = e^x + C, integral of a^x dx = a^x/ln(a) + C.
formula_latex: \int \sin x\, dx = -\cos x + C, \quad \int e^x\, dx = e^x + C, \quad \int \sec^2 x\, dx = \tan x + C
example: Find integral of (e^x + 3*sin(x)) dx = e^x - 3*cos(x) + C.

title: Integration by Substitution (u-substitution)
content: If the integrand contains a composite function f(g(x))*g'(x), substitute u = g(x), du = g'(x) dx to simplify the integral to integral of f(u) du. After integration, substitute back u = g(x). This reverses the chain rule of differentiation.
formula_latex: \int f(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)\, dx = \int f(u)\, du \quad \text{where } u = g(x)
example: Find integral of 2x*cos(x^2) dx. Let u = x^2, du = 2x dx. Integral becomes integral of cos(u) du = sin(u) + C = sin(x^2) + C.

title: Integration by Parts
content: Integration by parts formula: integral of u dv = uv - integral of v du. Choose u and dv using the LIATE rule (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential). The function earlier in LIATE is typically chosen as u. Sometimes the method must be applied twice.
formula_latex: \int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du
example: Find integral of x*e^x dx. Let u = x (algebraic), dv = e^x dx. Then du = dx, v = e^x. integral = x*e^x - integral of e^x dx = x*e^x - e^x + C = e^x(x - 1) + C.

title: Integration by Partial Fractions
content: A proper rational function P(x)/Q(x) (degree P < degree Q) can be decomposed into simpler fractions. For distinct linear factors: A/(x-a) + B/(x-b). For repeated factors: A/(x-a) + B/(x-a)^2. For irreducible quadratic factors: (Ax+B)/(x^2+px+q). Each simpler fraction is then integrated separately.
formula_latex: \frac{P(x)}{(x-a)(x-b)} = \frac{A}{x-a} + \frac{B}{x-b}

title: Trigonometric Substitution
content: For integrands involving sqrt(a^2 - x^2), use x = a*sin(theta). For sqrt(a^2 + x^2), use x = a*tan(theta). For sqrt(x^2 - a^2), use x = a*sec(theta). These substitutions convert the square root into a trigonometric expression that simplifies the integral.
formula_latex: \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}: x = a\sin\theta, \quad \sqrt{a^2 + x^2}: x = a\tan\theta, \quad \sqrt{x^2 - a^2}: x = a\sec\theta
example: Find integral of 1/sqrt(4 - x^2) dx. Let x = 2*sin(theta), dx = 2*cos(theta) d(theta). sqrt(4 - 4sin^2(theta)) = 2*cos(theta). Integral = integral of 2*cos(theta)/(2*cos(theta)) d(theta) = theta + C = arcsin(x/2) + C.

title: Integration Using Special Forms
content: Standard results for special forms: integral of dx/(x^2 + a^2) = (1/a)*arctan(x/a) + C. Integral of dx/(x^2 - a^2) = (1/2a)*ln|(x-a)/(x+a)| + C. Integral of dx/sqrt(x^2 + a^2) = ln|x + sqrt(x^2 + a^2)| + C. These are frequently tested in JEE.
formula_latex: \int \frac{dx}{x^2 + a^2} = \frac{1}{a}\arctan\frac{x}{a} + C, \quad \int \frac{dx}{x^2 - a^2} = \frac{1}{2a}\ln\left|\frac{x-a}{x+a}\right| + C
example: Find integral of dx/(x^2 + 9). Here a^2 = 9, a = 3. Answer: (1/3)*arctan(x/3) + C.

title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
content: Part 1: If f is continuous on [a, b] and F(x) = integral from a to x of f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(x). Part 2: If F is any antiderivative of f on [a, b], then integral from a to b of f(x) dx = F(b) - F(a). This theorem connects differentiation and integration.
formula_latex: \int_a^b f(x)\, dx = F(b) - F(a) \quad \text{where } F'(x) = f(x)
example: Evaluate integral from 0 to pi of sin(x) dx. Antiderivative of sin(x) is -cos(x). = [-cos(x)] from 0 to pi = -cos(pi) - (-cos(0)) = -(-1) + 1 = 2.

title: Properties of Definite Integrals
content: Key properties: (1) integral from a to a of f(x) dx = 0. (2) integral from a to b = -integral from b to a. (3) integral from a to b = integral from a to c + integral from c to b for a < c < b. (4) integral from 0 to a of f(x) dx = integral from 0 to a of f(a-x) dx (King's property). (5) For even f: integral from -a to a = 2*integral from 0 to a. For odd f: integral from -a to a = 0.
formula_latex: \int_0^a f(x)\, dx = \int_0^a f(a-x)\, dx, \quad \int_{-a}^a f(x)\, dx = \begin{cases} 2\int_0^a f(x)\,dx & f \text{ even} \\ 0 & f \text{ odd} \end{cases}
example: Evaluate integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin(x)/(sin(x)+cos(x)) dx. Let I = the integral. By King's property: I = integral from 0 to pi/2 of cos(x)/(cos(x)+sin(x)) dx. Adding: 2I = integral from 0 to pi/2 of 1 dx = pi/2. So I = pi/4.

title: Area Under a Curve
content: The area bounded by y = f(x), the x-axis, and the lines x = a, x = b is given by the integral from a to b of |f(x)| dx. If f(x) >= 0 on [a, b], area = integral from a to b of f(x) dx. For area between two curves y = f(x) and y = g(x) where f >= g, area = integral from a to b of [f(x) - g(x)] dx.
formula_latex: A = \int_a^b |f(x)|\, dx, \quad A_{\text{between}} = \int_a^b [f(x) - g(x)]\, dx \text{ where } f(x) \geq g(x)
example: Find the area enclosed between y = x^2 and y = x. Set x^2 = x, so x = 0 and x = 1. For 0 <= x <= 1, x >= x^2. Area = integral from 0 to 1 of (x - x^2) dx = [x^2/2 - x^3/3] from 0 to 1 = 1/2 - 1/3 = 1/6.

title: Walli's Formula and Reduction Formulas
content: Walli's formula gives: integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin^n(x) dx = integral from 0 to pi/2 of cos^n(x) dx = [(n-1)/n]*[(n-3)/(n-2)]*...*[1/2]*(pi/2) for even n, and [(n-1)/n]*[(n-3)/(n-2)]*...*[2/3]*1 for odd n. These are useful for JEE problems involving powers of trig functions.
formula_latex: \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\, dx = \frac{(n-1)!!}{n!!} \cdot \begin{cases} \frac{\pi}{2} & n \text{ even} \\ 1 & n \text{ odd} \end{cases}
example: Find integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin^4(x) dx. n = 4 (even). = (3/4)*(1/2)*(pi/2) = 3*pi/16.

title: Order and Degree of Differential Equations
content: The order of a differential equation (DE) is the order of the highest derivative present. The degree is the exponent of the highest order derivative after the DE is made free of radicals and fractions involving derivatives. The degree is defined only when the DE is a polynomial in its derivatives.
formula_latex: \text{Order: highest derivative order}, \quad \text{Degree: power of highest order derivative (polynomial form)}
example: Find order and degree of (d^2y/dx^2)^3 + (dy/dx)^2 + sin(dy/dx) = 0. Order = 2 (highest derivative is d^2y/dx^2). Degree is not defined because of sin(dy/dx) - the equation is not polynomial in derivatives.

title: Variable Separable Method
content: A first-order ODE dy/dx = f(x,y) is variable separable if it can be written as g(y) dy = h(x) dx. Integrate both sides separately: integral of g(y) dy = integral of h(x) dx + C. This is the simplest method for solving first-order ODEs.
formula_latex: \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{h(x)}{g(y)} \implies \int g(y)\, dy = \int h(x)\, dx + C
example: Solve dy/dx = xy. Separate: dy/y = x dx. Integrate: ln|y| = x^2/2 + C. So y = A*e^(x^2/2) where A = e^C.

title: Linear Differential Equation (First Order)
content: A first-order linear ODE has the form dy/dx + P(x)*y = Q(x). The solution is found using the integrating factor (IF) = e^(integral of P(x) dx). Multiply both sides by IF: d/dx[IF * y] = IF * Q(x). Then y * IF = integral of [IF * Q(x)] dx + C.
formula_latex: \frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x), \quad \text{IF} = e^{\int P(x)\, dx}, \quad y \cdot \text{IF} = \int Q(x) \cdot \text{IF}\, dx + C
example: Solve dy/dx + y = e^x. P(x) = 1, IF = e^(integral of 1 dx) = e^x. Multiply: e^x*dy/dx + e^x*y = e^(2x). d/dx[y*e^x] = e^(2x). Integrate: y*e^x = e^(2x)/2 + C. So y = e^x/2 + C*e^(-x).

title: Homogeneous Differential Equations
content: A first-order ODE dy/dx = f(x,y) is homogeneous if f(tx, ty) = f(x,y) for all t, i.e., f depends only on y/x. Substitute y = vx, so dy/dx = v + x*dv/dx. This reduces the equation to a variable separable form in v and x.
formula_latex: \frac{dy}{dx} = F\left(\frac{y}{x}\right): \text{ let } y = vx \implies v + x\frac{dv}{dx} = F(v)
example: Solve dy/dx = (x + y)/x = 1 + y/x. Let y = vx, dy/dx = v + x*dv/dx. So v + x*dv/dx = 1 + v, giving x*dv/dx = 1, dv = dx/x. Integrate: v = ln|x| + C. So y/x = ln|x| + C, i.e., y = x*ln|x| + Cx.

title: Concavity and Points of Inflection
content: A function is concave up (convex) where f''(x) > 0 and concave down where f''(x) < 0. A point of inflection is where concavity changes, i.e., f''(x) changes sign. At a point of inflection, f''(c) = 0 or f''(c) is undefined, but f''(x) = 0 alone is not sufficient.
formula_latex: f''(x) > 0 \implies \text{concave up}, \quad f''(x) < 0 \implies \text{concave down}, \quad \text{inflection at sign change of } f''
example: Find inflection points of f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2. f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x, f''(x) = 6x - 6 = 0 gives x = 1. f''(x) < 0 for x < 1 (concave down), f''(x) > 0 for x > 1 (concave up). Inflection at x = 1, f(1) = 0.

title: Leibniz Rule for Differentiation Under the Integral Sign
content: If F(x) = integral from u(x) to v(x) of f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(v(x))*v'(x) - f(u(x))*u'(x). When the limits are constants and f depends on x as a parameter, d/dx integral from a to b of f(x,t) dt = integral from a to b of partial f/partial x dt. This is a powerful technique for JEE advanced problems.
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}\int_{u(x)}^{v(x)} f(t)\, dt = f(v(x)) \cdot v'(x) - f(u(x)) \cdot u'(x)
example: If F(x) = integral from 0 to x^2 of sin(t) dt, find F'(x). Here u(x) = 0, v(x) = x^2, f(t) = sin(t). F'(x) = sin(x^2) * 2x - sin(0) * 0 = 2x*sin(x^2).
